title: Creatine for Women: The Complete Evidence-Based Guide (2026)
description: Women's creatine research through menopause, training, body composition, and mood. No bloating myths, no men's-magazine claims — just the evidence.
target_queries: does creatine cause weight gain in women
key_points: Women have ~70-80% lower baseline muscle creatine than men (Forsberg et al., 1991), so response to supplementation may be larger
Smith-Ryan et al. 2021 (Nutrients) — comprehensive review of creatine benefits across female lifespan: strength, body composition, bone health, mood, cognitive function
Chilibeck et al. 2017 (Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise) — creatine + resistance training reduced bone mineral density loss in postmenopausal women
Gordon et al. 2018 — creatine may improve mood and reduce depression symptoms in women, particularly effective when combined with SSRIs
Dispel bloating myth — water retention is INTRA-cellular (inside muscle cells), not sub-cutaneous puffiness. Visible weight gain 1-2 lbs is muscle cell hydration
Dose is same as men (3-5g daily) — no adjustment for body weight needed at maintenance
Discuss pregnancy/breastfeeding: insufficient human data, consult OB-GYN (Dickinson et al. 2014 animal data on fetal neuroprotection is promising but not clinically actionable)
Hormonal contraceptives don't affect creatine absorption or efficacy
Creatine works particularly well paired with resistance training (not cardio alone)
sections_to_cover: Why Women Respond Differently to Creatine (mechanism, baseline levels)
What the Research Actually Shows (Smith-Ryan 2021, Chilibeck 2017, Gordon 2018)
Body Composition: Does Creatine Make Women 'Bulky'? (debunk)

How to Take It: Dose, Timing, Forms (all same as men)
Common Concerns: Bloating, Water Weight, Hair Loss (spoiler: no, no, probably not)

title: Best Time to Take Magnesium: The Timing Science (2026)
description: Morning, evening, or with food — when you take magnesium matters more than you think. Form-specific timing rules backed by clinical research.
key_points: Glycinate and threonate: take 30-60 min before bed for sleep benefit (glycine has calming effect, threonate supports cognition with overnight consolidation)
Citrate: morning or early afternoon — mild laxative effect makes evening dosing impractical for most
Oxide: skip entirely (~4% absorption per Firoz & Graber 2001)
Take with food if sensitive stomach — slightly reduces absorption but reduces GI issues
Space from iron, calcium, and certain antibiotics by at least 2 hours
Split doses over 200mg into morning + evening to improve absorption
If taking for muscle cramps during exercise, take 1-2 hours before activity
Abbasi et al. 2012 sleep study used bedtime dosing — supports pre-bed timing for glycinate
Don't take with coffee (tannins may slightly impair absorption; wait 1 hour)
Magnesium threonate (Magtein) — evidence for cognitive benefits at split AM/PM dosing per Liu 2016
sections_to_cover: Why Magnesium Timing Actually Matters (absorption + form-specific effects)
Morning vs Evening: It Depends on the Form

title: Alpha-GPC vs Citicoline: Which Choline Source Actually Works?
description: Two of the most effective nootropics, both precursors to acetylcholine. We break down the evidence, dosing, bioavailability, and which one fits which goal.
target_queries: which is better alpha gpc or citicoline
key_points: Both are phospholipid choline sources that cross the blood-brain barrier efficiently (unlike choline bitartrate or CDP-choline)
Alpha-GPC: ~40% elemental choline by weight. Crosses BBB fast, raises brain acetylcholine within 30-60 min
Citicoline (CDP-choline): ~18% elemental choline by weight, but provides cytidine which converts to uridine (phospholipid synthesis)
Alpha-GPC — better evidence for acute cognitive performance, power output in athletes (Bellar 2015)
Citicoline — better evidence for long-term memory, age-related cognitive decline, stroke recovery
Dose: Alpha-GPC 300-600mg, Citicoline 250-500mg
Side effects: Alpha-GPC more likely to cause headaches (fat-soluble, fast BBB crossing); Citicoline very well-tolerated
2021 observational concern about Alpha-GPC and increased stroke risk (Lee et al.) — small association, unclear causation, flag for cautious use if cardiovascular risk
Cost: Citicoline typically more expensive per mg, but dose is lower so per-serving cost is similar
Can be stacked together for broader coverage — common advanced nootropics stack
sections_to_cover: The Choline Problem: Why Form Matters

title: Best Adaptogens for Stress: Ashwagandha vs Rhodiola vs Reishi
description: Three of the most-studied adaptogens, compared head-to-head on clinical evidence, mechanism, and which one fits which stress profile.
key_points: Ashwagandha (KSM-66, 600mg/day): strongest evidence for cortisol reduction (~28% vs placebo, Chandrasekhar 2012), anxiety, sleep onset. Best for chronic stress + sleep issues
Rhodiola (3% rosavins, 200-600mg): best for acute stress, fatigue, cognitive performance under pressure. Kasper 2017 — significant burnout symptom reduction in 12 weeks
Reishi (Ganoderma lucidum, hot water extract 1-1.5g): weakest adaptogen evidence but good for immune modulation + sleep support. Wachtel-Galor 2011 safety data
Ashwagandha acts primarily via HPA axis (cortisol), Rhodiola via SAM/PEA/serotonin, Reishi via immune cytokine modulation
Onset: Rhodiola fastest (30-60 min acute effects), Ashwagandha requires 4-8 weeks, Reishi subtle over weeks
Cycling: Rhodiola may be best cycled (4 weeks on / 2 off) due to potential tolerance; Ashwagandha and Reishi can be daily long-term
Side effects: Ashwagandha GI/drowsiness (flag liver concerns), Rhodiola mild insomnia if taken late, Reishi generally very mild
Not true adaptogens but sometimes grouped: holy basil, eleuthero, schisandra — briefly note

title: Creatine and Hair Loss: What the 2009 Study Actually Says
description: The only study linking creatine to hair loss measured a DHT proxy, not actual hair loss — and has never been replicated. Here's what the evidence really shows.
target_queries: creatine for men with male pattern baldness
key_points: The entire creatine-hair-loss concern traces to ONE study: van der Merwe, Brooks, Myburgh (2009, Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine)
That study measured DHT (dihydrotestosterone) ratios, not actual hair loss — 20 rugby players, 7 days loading + 14 days maintenance
DHT increased by ~56% (within normal clinical range throughout), DHT:T ratio increased by 36%
Has never been replicated — 15+ years, zero follow-up confirmation
DHT increase does NOT automatically equal hair loss — requires genetic predisposition to androgenic alopecia (AGA) plus scalp follicle sensitivity
Antonio et al. 2024 reviewed all creatine DHT studies — concluded evidence for hair loss is 'extremely limited and speculative'
If you have family history of MPB: creatine may theoretically accelerate existing pattern, but clinical evidence is absent
Topical finasteride or minoxidil works alongside creatine — no documented negative interactions
Not a reason to stop taking creatine for the 99% of users without genetic predisposition
Honest take: if you're already using finasteride/minoxidil, keep taking creatine; if you're concerned and have strong MPB family history, you can skip without losing much

title: Vitamin D3 vs D2: The Science Behind the 87% Gap
description: D3 raises blood levels 87% more effectively than D2 — and your prescription vitamin D might be the inferior form. Here's what to know.
key_points: Tripkovic et al. 2012 meta-analysis (American Journal of Clinical Nutrition) — pooled RCT data showing D3 is ~87% more effective at raising serum 25(OH)D than D2
D3 (cholecalciferol) — what your skin makes from sunlight, what's in most animal-based foods, most OTC supplements
D2 (ergocalciferol) — derived from yeast/fungi, what prescription 50,000 IU weekly pills typically contain, cheaper to produce
D3 binds more efficiently to vitamin D binding protein in blood, has longer half-life (~24 hours vs 6-8 hours for D2)
D2 clears faster and is less efficiently converted to 25(OH)D in liver
Vegans: lichen-sourced D3 now widely available — no longer forced to take D2
If your doctor prescribed 50,000 IU weekly D2: ask about switching to daily D3 — many clinicians still prescribe D2 out of habit
Equivalent doses: roughly 1 unit D3 ≈ 1.87 units D2 in raising blood levels
Combine D3 with K2 (MK-7) at doses above 2,000 IU/day (Knapen 2017)
Take with fat-containing meal — absorption up to 50% better (Dawson-Hughes 2015)
